text: CHETECAN, a town and harbour of the island of Cape Breton, on the NW coast, to the E of Cape Beauque.
CHETIGUA, a town of Gangetic India, in the prov. and 50 m. SSW of Tenasserim, on a small river which flows into the Indian ocean, opposite the Dome island.

CHETIMACHES, a lake of the state of Louisiana, U. S., between the Atchafalaya, with which it inosculates by several intermediate streams, and the Teche rivers. It is 40 m. in length, and varies from 1 to 6 m. in breadth, and is very shallow. The surrounding country is low and marshy, and is annually overflowed. - A tribe of Indians who inhabit the banks of the Teche, near its mouth, bear this name.
CHETNOLE, a chapelry in the p. of Yetminster of Dorset, 61 m. SSW of Sherborne. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 222.
CHETTISHAM, a chapelry in the p. of St. Mary, Ely, Cambridgeshire. Pop. 90.
CHETTLE, a parish of Dorset, 61 m. NE of Blandford Forum. Area 1,510 acres. Pop. 122.
CHETTON, a parish of Salop, 4 m. WSW of Bridgenorth. Area 7,140 acres. Pop. 693.
CHETWODE, a parish of Buckinghamshire. 9 m. WNW of Winslow. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 197.
CHETWYND, a parish of Salop, 13 m. N of Newport, on the Mees. Area 3,330 acres. Pop. 740.
CHETWYND, a township in the p. of Edgmond, Salop, about a mile S of Newport. Pop. 385.
CHEUX, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Calvados, cant. of Tilly-sur-Seulles, 9 m. W of Caen, on the r. bank of the Mue. Pop. 1,009.

CHEVAGNES, or CHEVANNES, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Allier, arrond. of Moulins. The cant. comprises 10 com. Pop. in 1831, 7,133; in 1841, 7,507. The village is on the 1. bank of the Acolin, 12 m. ENE of Moulins. Pop. 858. Fairs are held three times a-year.
CHEVAIGNE', a commune of France, in the dep. and 16 m. NE of Mayenne, cant. of Couptrain. Pop. 1,240.
CHEVAL-BLANC (LE), a commune of France, in the dep. of Vaucluse, cant. of Cavaillon, near the r. bank of the Durance, 19 m. SE of Avignon. Pop. 1,530.

CHEVALIER, an island of France, in the dep. of Finistere, cant. of Pont-l'Abbé, 11 m. SSW of Quimper, in a lake formed by the river Pont l'Abbé. It is about 3 m. in circumference, and is very fertile.
CHEVANCEAUX, a commune of France, in the dep. of Charente-Inferieure, cant. of Montlieu, 16 m. SE of Jonzac. Pop. 1,346.
CHEVELEY, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 34 m. SE of Newmarket. Area 2,489 acres. Pop. 645.
CHEVELINKA, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 42 m. SSE of Kharkov, district and 20 m. S of Zmiev.
CHEVENEY, or KEVENACH, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Berne, 4 m. WSW of Porentruy. Pop. 272.
CHEVENING, a parish of Kent, 31 m. NW of Seven-Oaks. Area 3,550 acres. Pop. 1,003.
CHEVERELL (GREAT), a parish of Wilts, 1 m. W of West Lavington. Area 1,840 acres. Pop. 557.
CHEVERELL (LITTLE), a parish of Wilts, 13 m. W of West Lavington. Area 1,930 acres. Pop. 295.
CHEVERMEH, a village of Persia, in Kurdistan, 3 m. NW of Mush.
CHEVERNY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loir-et-Cher, cant. of Contres, 10 m. SE of Blois, on the l. bank of the Conon. Pop. 1,021.
CHEVET, a township in the p. of Roystone, W. R. of Yorkshire, 41 m. SSE of Wakefield, on the York and Derby railway. Area 850 acres. Pop. 52.
CHEVILLON, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of the Haute-Marne, arrond. of Vassy. The cant. comprises 15 com. Pop. in 1831, 5,906; in 1841, 6,414. The town is 12 m. ENE of Vassy. Pop. 991.
CHEVILLY, or LANGENNERIE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loiret, cant. of Artenay, 12 m. N of Orleans. Pop. 1,281.

CHEVINAY, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Vaugneray, 12 m. W of Lyon. Pop. 559. It contains the ruins of some Roman aqueducts, and several copper-mines that have been wrought from a remote period.
CHEVINGTON, a parish of Suffolk, 5 m. WSW of St. Edmund's-Bury. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 624. CHE the p. of Warkworth, Northumberland. Pop. of former, 289; of latter, 67.

CHEVIOT FELLS, a mountain range of South Africa, on the N frontier of the county of Somerset, between the Groen-Bergen on the W, and the Winterberg or Hyndhope-fells on the E. The head streams of the Baviaans river flowing through Glen Linden, descend from it on the S, and the Ganna Hock and other head-streams of the Tarka, issue from its N side, in the co. of Cradock.

CHEVIOT HILLS, a range of porphyritic hills, occupying a circle of about 15 m. in diam., on the border of England and Scotland, but chiefly within Northumberland, between the Scottish border on the NW, and the upper part of the river Coquet on the S. Some regard this chain as commencing at Loch Ryan on the W, and extending, with occasional interruptions, to the head of the Northumberland Beaumont; but the Cheviots, commonly so called, lie on the borders of Roxburgh and Northumberland, and may be regarded as commencing, on the east, with Cheviot hill, in N lat. 55° 29', 19 m. from Sunderland-point. This hill, the highest in the range, has an alt. of 2,684 ft., according to Sir Thomas Brisbane, or of 2,695 ft. according to Messrs. Adie and Galbreath. From this point, the C. run in a SW direction, by Carter Fell, alt. 2.020 ft., to Peel fell, in N lat. 55° 17', and W long. 2° 35'. The principal pass in the range is that known by the name of Carter-bar, by which the road from Jedburgh to Newcastle enters England. Many of these hills are of a conical form, some of them being perfect cones; almost all are pointed and finely formed, with smooth steep sides, their bases being separated only by deep, narrow, and sequestered glens. The soil on the lower slopes is fertile, producing a rich, close, green sward; but on the higher acclivities, patches of heath, points of rocks, and loose stones appear. Their only produce is grass; but they are the most valuable of the mountain tracts in this vicinity, being depastured by numerous flocks of the valuable and peculiar breed of sheep termed Cheviots, now widely diffused not only over the N of England, but also over almost all Scotland. Goats also are kept among them, and they abound with grouse. On the top of the mountain, called by way of eminence, the Cheviot, there are extensive tracts of heath, and a large loch which is often frozen at midsummer.
CHEVIRE'-LE-ROUGE, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Maine-et-Loire, cant. and 5 m. NW of Baugé. Pop. 1,741.
CHEVLI, a village of Persia, in the pashalik of Diyarbekir, in the district of Jabukjur, 35 m. ENE of Pelu, on a branch of the Murad-chai.
CHEVRE, a hamlet of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, cant. of Moulins-en-Gilbert, com. of Vandenesse. Pop. 70. It contains a blast furnace and extensive iron forges.

CHEVREUSE, a canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of the Seine-et-Oise, arrond. of Rambouillet. The cant. comprises 21 com. Pop. in 1831, 9,181; in 1841, 9,863. The town, which is small, is situated on the Yvette, in a fine valley of the same name, 12 m. ENE of Rambouillet. Pop. 1,730. It contains an hospital and several tanneries, and has a considerable trade in cattle, wool, mercery, and earthen-ware. Fairs are held 4 times a-year. In the environs is a brick-work. C. was formerly fortified; and on an adjacent height commanding the town are the ruins of a fortress. It was taken in 1414 by the duke of Burgundy, three years afterwards by Tanneguy-du-Châtel, and subsequently by the English.
CHEVRIE'RE, a commune of France, in the dep.
CHEVINGTON (East and West), townships in of the Loire, cant. of Saint-Galmier. Pop. 1,454.
CHEVROLLIE'RE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Loire-Inferieure, cant. of Saint- Philbert. Pop. 1,655.
CHEVROUX, or CHEYRES, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, on the E coast of the lake of Neufchatel, 5 m. NE of Estavayer. Pop. 313, Catholics.
CHEW'S LANDING, a village in Gloucester co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 38 m. SSE of Trenton. on the N branch of Big-Timber creek.
CHEWLIA, a town of India, in the prov. of Allahabad, district of Gurramundlah, on the r. bank of the Nerbudda, 30 m. S by E of Gurrah.
CHEW-MAGNA, or BISHOP-CHEW, a parish and village in Somersetshire, 3 m. W of Pensford, on a branch of the Avon. Area of p. 4,490 acres. Pop. 2,096.
CHEW-STOKE, a parish in Somersetshire, 42 m. SW of Pensford. Area 1,910 acres. Pop. 825.
CHEWTON-MENDIP, a parish and village in Somersetshire, 6 m. NNE of Wells, on the Mendip hills. Area of p. 7,020 acres. Pop. 1,216.
CHEXBRES, a parish and village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Vaud, 10 m. E of Lausanne. Pop. 727.
CHEY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Sevres, cant. of Lezay. Pop. 1,262.
CHEYLADE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Cantal, cant. of Murat. Pop. 2,127.
CHE-YANG-PAOU, a town of China, in the prov. of Fo-kyen, in N lat. 25° 34′ 48″, long. 3° 41′ 30″ E of Pekin.
CHEYE, a town of S Africa, in the Barolong territory, on the Maretsawney river, in S lat. 25° 50'.
CHEYNE (POINT), a low rocky point on the S coast of Melville peninsula, in N lat. 66° 9', W long. 84° 30′.
CHEYNOW, a town of Bohemia, in the circle and 6 m. E of Tabor. There are mineral springs in the neighbourhood.

CHE'ZE (LA), a canton, commune, and village of France, in the dep. of Cotes-du-Nord, arrond. of Loudeac.—The cant. comprises 7 com. Pop. in 1831, 11,240.—The com. and village are 6 m. SE of Loudeac, on the Lié. Pop. 405.
CHEZEAUX (LES), a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Vienne, cant. of Saint-Sulpice-les- Feuilles. Pop. 1,792.
CHEZERY, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Ain, cant. of Collonges. Pop. 1,205.
CHEZY-L'ABBAYE, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Charly, 4 m. S of Chateau-Thierry. Pop. 1,314.
CHIA, a town of Spain, in the prov. and 48 m. NE of Huesca, in the partido of Boltana.
CHIABRANO, a village of the Sardinian states, in the division of Turin, 9 m. NW of Pignerol. Good wine is made here.
CHIADU, a town of Yarriba in NW Africa, 170 m. NW of Benin, in N lat. 8° 24'.
CHIAJANO, a village of Naples, in the prov. and 3 m. NW of Naples, cant. of Marano. Pop. 1,200.
CHIAMPO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Venice, deleg. and 14 m. W of Vicenza, on the r. bank of the Aldego. Pop. 3,500.

CHIA'NA (VAL DI), a flat and nearly level valley of Italy, which stretches among the Apennines, from the Arno near Arezzo, to the Tiber near Orvieto, between two ridges of hills, one of which divides it from the valley of the Ombrone on the W, and the other from the valley of the Tiber on the E. It is a marshy district, and a part of its waters drain off N into the Arno, by a stream which is known as the C. Toscana; while another portion flows S towards the Tiber under the name of the C. Pontificia. It should seem that the greater part of the waters of this valley flow- CHI ed, in ancient times, into the Tiber; but the Arno now receives the principal contribution from this quarter. The old road from Rome to Florence, the Via Cassia, ran through this valley in ancient times, and so late as the year 1,000; but the waters flowing towards the Tiber becoming gradually blocked up by their own deposits, the whole valley became a pestiferous marsh, no longer traversable by a road. Pope Clement undertook to drain that portion of it which belongs to the States-of-the-Church, and the work of elevating the land above the level of the water, by means of alluvial deposits, has been carried on with little intermission during the last three centuries. The mountain-streams flowing into the valley at certain seasons carry from 3 to 9 parts of earth in 100 of water; and this water was detained between artificial embankments till it became clear, or had deposited its sediment over the marshes. By this occasional folding of water the general level of the valley has been raised about 8 ft., and the whole accumulation is estimated at 880,000 cubic yards of earth. The C. Pontificia, forming the drain of this end of the valley, rises within a short distance of the C. Toscana; runs SSW; receives the Astrone on the r. bank; turns SSE near Orvieto, and, after a course of about 30 m., joins the Paglia on the I. bank, a little above its union with the Tiber. The same mode of treatment was adopted in the Tuscan part of the valley, by drawing off the waters of the C. after they had been made by temporary detention to deposit the earthy materials they carried with them. The C. Toscana is formed by the union of the Tresa, the Maranzano, and the Montelungo. After having deposited their sediment in Lake Chiusi, these streams flow under the name of the Canale-del-Passo-allo-Querce, into Lake Montepulciano; from which they again issue under the name of Canale-Maestro-de-la-C. Toscana, and join the Arno, on the l. bank, 6 m. NW of Arezzo, after a course of 42 m. navigable throughout. The fall of the C. Toscana is 15 toises = 57 ft. A huge weir was erected, under the superintendance of Dutch engineers, for the purpose of regulating and retarding at will the discharge of the C. into the Arno. The proposal to pull down or lower this dam, so as to obtain for the main stream of the C. a greater fall and more rapid course, was warmly opposed by the celebrated engineer Fossombrone, on account of the danger of inundation to which it would expose Florence and the Val d'Arno.

CHIANCIANO, a town of Tuscany, in the comp. of Arezzo, 4 m SE of Montepulciano, in the valley of the Chiana. Pop. 2,036. Gypsum is extensively dug here. About 1 m. to the NE are celebrated hotsprings, the Bagni-di-Sellana of the Middle ages.
CHIANNI-DE-REVALTE, a parish and village of Tuscany, in the comp. and 19 m. SE of Pisa, at an alt. of 950 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 1,552.

CHIANTE (IL), a region of Tuscany, comprising the basins of the Arno and the Ombrone, and the upper part of the valleys of the Elsa, the Pese, the Greve, and the Arbia. It is celebrated for its wines.

CHIANTLA, a town of Guatimala, in the prov. of Totonicapan, 130 m. NNW of Guatimala, on a river of the same name. Pop. under 1,000. Grapes, oranges, figs, and pears, are grown in the gardens of C.; but the chief article of commerce is lead, which is obtained from a rich mine in the vicinity.—The C. river rises in the volcanic chain of Central Guatimala, and flows in a NW course to the Sumasinta, which it joins on the l. bank, in N lat. 17° 9', after a course of 150 m.

CHIAPA, or Las Chiapas, a prov. of Mexico, stretching along the W declivity of the table-land of Guatimala, and comprising a portion of the plain of Tabasco. It is bounded on the NW and N by Tabasco; on the E by Guatimala; on the S by Soconusco; and on the W by Oaxaca. Its area is estimated at 38,500 sq. m. The pop. in 1837 was supposed to be 92,000. A large proportion of the surface is mountainous, and clothed with dense forests. The principal rivers are the Zeldales, the Chiantla, the Sumasinta, and the Tabasco. The climate is moist and warm, and the soil fertile. The principal article of export is logwood, which is floated down the Usumasinta and Tabasco. This prov. was formerly an intendancy of Guatimala, and comprised the government of Soconusco. Lying contiguous alike to Mexico and the federal states of Central America, it was claimed by both; and the option being given to the inhabitants of determining to which federation they would adhere, C. declared its wish to join the Mexican union, while the district of Soconusco adhered to the Central federation.-Near the village of Palenque in this prov. are some extraordinary ruins called Las casas de Piedras, or 'the Stone houses,' which mark the site of what must once have been a large and opulent city. "This metropolis," says Juarros, "concealed for ages in the midst of a vast desert, remained unknown until the middle of the 18th cent., when some Spaniards having penetrated the dreary solitude, found themselves within sight of the remains of what had once been a superb city, 6 leagues in circumf." Mr. Stephens, in his Incidents of Travel in Central America, has given a most interesting account of these ruins, and illustrated his explorations with numerous drawings of the principal edifices. See Palenque. CHIAPA-DE-LOS-ESPANOLES, OF CIUDAD REAL, the capital of the above prov., is situated in the plain of Gueizacatlan, in N lat. 16° 35', W long. 94° 16', on the Zeldales river, 300 m. NW of Guatimala. Pop. 5,000, of whom about one-eighth are Indians. It has some commerce in cocoa, sugar, cochinella, cotton, and wool.

CHIAPA-DE-LOS-INDIOS, a town in the same prov. of Chiapa, 36 m. W of the above, on the l. bank of the Tabasco, near the centre of the isthmus of Tehuantepec, in N lat. 17° 5', W long. 95° 53'. It is chiefly inhabited by Indians, who are said to amount to nearly 4,000 families, and who conduct an active commerce in sugar and logwood.
CHIAPUT, a town of Sinde, 50 m. S of Khyerpur, in N. lat. 26° 52'.

CHIARAMONTE, a town of Sardinia, in the prov. and 20 m. ENE of Sassari, on a mountain of the same name. Pop. 1,600.—Also a town of Sicily, in the prov. of Syracuse, 15 m. NNW of Modica. Amico, in his topographical dictionary of Sicily, published in 1759, assigns a pop. of 6,222 to this town, and it was returned a few years ago at 6,594. It is a wellbuilt town.

CHIARAVALLE, a village of Lombardy, in the gov. and 3 m. SE of Milan.—Also a town of Naples, in Calabria-Ultra 2da, 18 m. SSW of Catanzaro. Pop. 3,107.—Also a town in the States-of-the-Church, 10 m. W of Ancona, on the r. bank of the Esino.
CHIARENTZA. See KLARENTZA.

CHIARI, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, and deleg of Brescia on the l. bank of the Oglio, 15 m. W by S of Brescia. Pop. 8,000. Silk is extensively grown in the environs. The town possesses some remains of its ancient fortifications. -Prince Eugene defeated Marshal Villeroi in the neighbourhood of C. on 1st September, 1701.
CHIAROMONTE, a town of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicata, in the district and 25 m. E of Lagonegro. Pop. 2,948. The neighbouring territory produces good wine and silk.
CHIASEM, or TSHIASEM, a town of Java, on the N coast, 60 m. E of Batavia. CHI

CHIA'VARI, a province in the Sardinian states, in the division of Genoa, on the Mediterranean. It is about 20 m. in length from NW to SE, and from 10 to 15 m. in breadth. Its area is estimated at 268 Italian sq. m. Of the surface one-fourth part is under cultivation; and nearly one-half is covered with forests. The general character of the country is mountainous. The principal streams are the Rapallo and the Vara. The chief agricultural productions are corn, wine, oil, and chestnuts. A considerable amount of cattle and sheep are reared. The prov. is divided into 8 mandamento, and 28 comuni.- The pop. was returned in 1842 at 107,953.—Its cap., of the same name, is a handsome town, at the head of the bay of Rapallo, 22 m. ESE of Genoa, at the embouchure of the Sturla. Pop. 10,619. There is an ecclesiastical seminary here, and a very handsome church. Its chief manufactures are lace, silk, oil, and wine. An important fishery of anchovies is conducted from its small port; and marble and slate are quarried in the neighbourhood.

CHIAVENNA, a town of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, deleg. of the Valteline, 20 m. NW of Sondrio, on the Maira, 8 m. above the point where that river flows into the lake of Como, and at an alt. of 1,090 ft. above sea-level. Pop. 3,000. The union of the two great roads across the Splugen and the Septimer at this point, renders it a place of active transit commerce; and it has some native trade in silk, wine, and cattle.—C. is an ancient, and was formerly a fortified town. Its castle is regarded as one of the keys of Italy on the side of Germany. From 1512 to 1797 the territory of C. belonged to the Swiss canton of the Grisons.
CHIBAY, a village of Peru, in the district and 20 m. S of Caillomas, in S lat. 15° 40'.
CHIBLAK, a village of Turkey in Asia, in the Anatolian sanjak of Biga, on a stream flowing W to the Kalifaith-Asmak river, 21 m. SE of Hissarjik or Ilium Novum.
CH'HUNTAUNMYU, a town of Burmah, on the E bank of the Bhanmo river, 30 m. NNE of Hentha.
CHIBUK-ABAD, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the sanjank and NE of Anguri, on the declivity of the Elma-tagh.
CHICA-BALAPUR, or CHINABALABURAM, a town of India, in the Mysore territory, 36 m. N by E of Bangalore.
CHICACOLE. See CICACOLE.

CHICAGO, a thriving town, the cap. of Cook co., in the state of Illinois, U.S., 204 m. NNE of Springfield, in N lat. 42° 0', W long. 87° 35'. It is well situated, on both sides of a river of the same name, m. above its entrance into the S end of Lake Michigan.—The river is from 50 to 75 yards wide at the town, with a depth of from 15 to 25 ft. Its mouth is barred, but a good artificial harbour has been formed by the construction of piers running into the lake on each side of the entrance of the river. It rises in two branches. The N has its sources on the N boundary of Illinois, and after a course of about 40 m. unites with the S branch, which is only 6 m. in length, just above the town of C. There is a natural portage of only 6 m. from the C. river to the Des Plaines river, a head-branch of the Illinois; and the level between the two rivers is so low that at high-water it is often overflowed, so that boats can pass from river to river. In fact the waters flowing into two oceans at immense distances apart, rise in an immense swamp or feeding lake, whose elevation probably does not exceed 5 or 6 ft. above the C. river, while the descent to the Des Plaines is still less considerable.—The town of C. is most advantageously situated for trade at the W termination of the great chain of lake and canal communication by which Lake Michigan is connected with the Atlantic at New York. The completion of the Canadian canal has likewise opened a channel of communication with the St. Lawrence, and steam-boats of the first class now travel, without break or transhipment, from Quebec to C.,—a distance of nearly 1,700 m. Buffalo is the point of junction of the two routes from New York and from Quebec. To this point the emigrant travels first by steamer, 150 m., up the Hudson to Albany, and thence on the Erie canal by a horse tow-boat to Buffalo, a journey of 360 m. more, which is not generally performed in less than 8 days, so that it may safely be computed that the whole journey occupies 9 to 10 days. From Quebec, on the other hand, passengers are conveyed in steamers direct to Lewiston, and thence by railroad to Buffalo 30 m., performing the entire journey in 3 days at the utmost; so that, in addition to the certainly greater comfort of this mode of travelling, about 6 days are saved, and the expense of provisions for that period is avoided. There is a railroad from Albany to Buffalo, but it is, of course, far too expensive a conveyance for the majority of emigrants. The length of the voyage from Buffalo to C. is above 1,200 m., in which the traveller is carried by long stretches to the W, the N, and the S, but never out of sight of land on Lake Erie, and not long on Huron and Michigan.-C. is also connected by canal with the river Illinois. The Illinois and Michigan canal is about 100 m. in length, with 15 locks, overcoming 140 ft. of lockage. It is 60 ft. wide at the top, and 6 ft. deep. The Central railroad through Michigan is already in operation, and another road is progressing which is to go round the head of Lake Michigan to C., all tending to increase the facilities and to make this route a great thoroughfare, from the Atlantic cities to the Mississippi. When the government expedition to the source of St. Peter's river visited this place in 1823, they arrived at the opinion that, "as a place of business, C. offers no inducement to the settler: for the whole annual amount of the trade on the lake did not exceed the cargo of 5 or 6 schooners." All the advantages enumerated have, however, given the town of C. an extraordinary impulse. In 1840, its pop. was 4,853; in 1848, it had increased to 19,724. In 1836 the value of imports into the town of C. amounted to 325,203 dollars, while the whole value of exports for the same year was only 1,000 d. In 1840, the imports had risen to 3,500,000 d. In 1848, the imports were 8,338,639 d., and the value of exports was estimated at 10,706,333 d. These consisted of 2,160,000 bushels of wheat, 45,200 barrels of flour, 59,200 barrels of beef and pork, and 961,400 lbs. of wool. Nor is its importance as a town likely to decrease, as the time is not far distant when, by a junction of that canal with the Wisconsin river, there will be a practicable navigation to the Mississippi, giving to this town and the state of Illinois the advantage of the New Orleans market on one side, and that of New York on the other. The country near C. is very flat and featureless. The lake is unchequered by islands; and the land scenery is equally undiversified, consisting of a level plain with a few patches of thin and scrubby wood scattered over it. It is one of the oldest settlements in the Indian country. Perrot, who visited the place in 1671, found 'Chicagou' the residence of a powerful chief of the Miamis.

CHICAMA, a town, or rather a collection of ranchos, of Peru, in the dep. of Truxillo, near the l. bank of a river of the same name, in S lat. 7° 52', 12 m. E by N of San Jago. About a m. to the E, is a large convent of the order of Merced.-The river rises on the W flank of the Nevado-de-Huavlillas, and has a circuitous course N, NW, and SW, to the Pacific. existing in England. It is an octagon in the de- II. CHI During the rains it discharges the greater part of its waters into the Pacific; but at other times they are all used for irrigation. Sugar and olives are cultivated in the valley of the river.

CHICAMOCHA, a river of New Granada, in the prov. of Santa Fe de Bogota, which rises near Tunja, under the name of the Gallinazos; flows NE at the base of the Lomas-del-Viento; turns W, and assumes in succession the name of the Sogamoso, the Chia, and the C.; and flows into the Magdalena under the parallel of 7° 10' N, after a course of 250 m. Its principal affluent is the Suarez.
CHICAMUGLUR, a town of India, in Mysore, 86 m. NW of Seringapatam, on a head-stream of the Hennavutti.
CHICANHUALLI, a town of India, in Mysore, 67 m. N of Seringapatam. It is strongly fortified.

CHICAPA, a river of Mexico, in the state of Oaxaca, rising on the S flank of the Sierra de la Madre, and flowing in a course from E to W, and then from N to S, into the Duic-quialoi, a lake to the E of Tehuantepec. At the Venta de C., where the road from the Portillo-de-Tarifa strikes it, this river has an alt. of 24 metres = 78 ft. above sea-level; near the rancho of Puerta-Vieja, of 272 ft.; and at the Ultimo rancho, of 682 ft. This river conveys a considerable body of water into the lake; and might be made an important feeder of the proposed canal across the isthmus of Tehuantepec.

CHICATI, a river of Mexico, in the state of Tabasco, a continuation of the Arroyo-de-Meluco, which inosculates with the Arroyo-de-Jaboncillo, flowing W into the Chilapa, and running NNW, is said to join the Usumasinta on the l. bank.
CHICHACOTTA, a town of Northern India, in Butan, in N lat. 26° 85′, 20 m. N of Cutch-Bahar.

CHICHAS, a district of Bolivia, in the intendancy of Potosi, stretching across the head-streams of the Pilcomayo to the frontiers of La Plata. Among the streams which intersect its surface is the C., a tributary of the San Juan.
CHICHAWUTNI, a village of Northern India, in the Punjab, on the l. bank of the Ravi, N lat. 30° 29′.
CHICHE', a town of France, in the dep. of Deux- Sevres, cant. and 6 m. SE of Bressuire, on the r. bank of the Thouaret. Pop. 1,124.
CHICHE'E, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Yonne, cant. of Chablis. Pop. 768.
CHICHELEY, a parish in Bucks, 25 m. NE of Newport-Pagnell. Area 1,620 acres. Pop. 256.

CHICHESTER, a city and county of itself, locally situated in the hund. of Box and Stockbridge, W division of Sussex, 56 m. SW by S of London, and about 6 m. from the coast, on a plain which extends from the W of Brighton to Portsmouth. The Lavant, a rivulet generally dry in the summer, nearly encircles the town, and falls into an estuary at Dell-key. There is a small harbour about 11 m. from the city, which communicates with it by means of a canal forming a branch of the Portsmouth and London canal. Vessels of 180 tons can come up at high tides to the quay of this harbour.—The city is generally well-built, paved, watered, and drained. The principal streets are spacious, and contain several large houses; but there is still room for considerable increase within the walls, which were originally strongly built, with four gates fronting the cardinal points of the compass. From these gates run four principal streets, named after their gates, which meet in its centre nearly at right angles to each other. In the centre of their point of intersection stands a cross, which is universally acknowledged to be one of the finest buildings of the kind $ corated style of English architecture, and having a large central column from which spring numerous bold ribs beneath a vaulted roof. Eight pier-buttresses support the superincumbent panelled wall, parapet, pinnacles, and flying buttresses; and the whole is surmounted by an open turret of modern and inferior workmanship. At one time the vicinity of this cross was used as a market-place; but the increased pop. requiring a more extensive area for the purpose, a large and convenient market house was erected in North-street. The gates of the city have long ago been removed, but the walls remain nearly perfect, and were, upwards of a century ago, formed into terraces covered with gravel, which are now shaded with rows of lofty elm-trees, and afford a delightful promenade. The Friary, near the N gate, is an ancient building. The chapel attached to this friary was converted into a guidhall, and is still used as such. The palace of the bishop is chiefly remarkable for its extensive and admirably planned gardens. The corn-exchange is an elegant building; and the theatre has some pretensions to elegance. The infirmary, a beautiful building, situated on the N slope outside the city, and surrounded with airing grounds for the patients, was erected by subscription in 1826. - The cathedral, erected in the 13th cent., is the most conspicuous edifice in the city. As a cathedral church it is of an inferior class; but it is a very beautiful edifice, built in the usual form of a cross, with a tower rising from the centre, surmounted by an octagonal spire, 300 ft. high. The cathedral is 410 ft. in length; and 227 ft. in breadth along the transepts. The gross yearly income of the see, on an average of three years, ending 1831, was £4,375. The persons composing the corporation are a dean and four canons residentiary, who divide the net income equally. C. is a diocese co-extensive with the county, 22 peculiars excepted. Some parts of the cathedral which had fallen into decay have been lately restored in a judicious manner.—The parochial churches are remarkable for the poverty of their appearance. The octagonal chapel of St. John, however, is a handsome structure; so also is the new Gothic church, erected without the walls in 1836. Besides the Established churches, there are here places of worship for Independents, Presbyterians, and Wesleyan Methodists. The area of the city and county is 1,680 acres. The pop. in 1739 was 4,030; in 1801, 4,744; in 1831, 8,270; and in 1841, 8,512. The trade of the town consists chiefly in malt, flour, corn, timber, coal, and wool. About two centuries ago C. nearly, if not wholly, monopolized the trade of needle-making in England. This trade was principally carried on in the parish of St. Pancras, without the E gate, where, before the civil war under Charles I., almost every house was occupied by a needle-maker; but in 1643 this quarter of the town was completely demolished; and though the houses were afterwards rebuilt, the trade itself was never perfectly restored. After the Revolution, manufactories of this article were established at Sheffield and Birmingham; and the business is now wholly extinct in this city. Large quantities of corn are exported through C. for London and the West of England. Salt is made at Itchenor, about 3 m. distant. Prawns and lobsters, caught at Selsea, about 7 m. distant, form considerable items in its commerce with London, which is greatly facilitated by means of the Portsmouth and Arundel canal, which passes on the S side of the town. The cattlemarket is one of the greatest held within the precincts of any city or town in England, London excepted. There are annual horse-races in the vicinity, at Goodward park, the seat of the duke of Richmond, which attract many spectators.—Since the 23° of Edward I. the city has sent two members to parliament,—a pri- CHI vilege which it still retains under the reform act. The number of electors under the new franchise in 1832 was 680; in 1837, 884; in 1847, 797. The boundary of the parl. borough was considerably enlarged round the city, especially in the W and S precincts, and in the vicinity of the Arundel canal basin. The pop. of the parl. borough in 1841 was 8,084. History.] C. is a place of great antiquity. It was unquestionably a Roman station, probably Regnum. At the Norman survey. C. consisted of only 238 houses, which were given to Roger de Montgomery, created first Earl of Chichester. The siege which it sustained under Charles I. seems to be the only circumstance worthy of particular record in its annals.

CHICHESTER, a township of Merrimac co., in the state of New Hampshire, U. S., 8 m. E of Concord. It presents a level surface, with a good and well-cultivated soil, and is watered by Suncook river, which affords good water-power. Pop. in 1840, 1,028. -Also a township of Lower Canada, extending NNE from the Ottawa or Grand river, opposite Alumet or Black island, and between Sheen and Watham townships. It is intersected by Black river.
CHICHIBACOA (CAPE), a headland of Venezuela, 13 m. NW by N of Point Espada, the W point of the entrance of the gulf of Maracaybo, in N lat. 12° 15½, W long. 71° 20'.
CHICHILIANE-EN-TRIE'VES, a village of France, in the dep. of the Isère, cant. of Clelles-en- Trièves, 30 m. SSW of Grenoble. Pop. 1,065. In the vicinity is a mine of lead.
CHICHIS, a town of Texas, in Jasper co., 35 m. SE of Nacogdoches.

CHICHUNDEE, a village of Afghanistan, on the road from Babur-ka-Killa to Dara-Ismail-Khan, at some distance from the r. bank of the Zhobe, and 15 m. WNW of the mountain of Tukti-Soliman, in N lat. 31° 42'.

CHICKAHOMINY, a river in the state of Virginia, U. S., which takes its rise 25 m. NW of Richmond; forms the boundary of several counties; and after a total course of about 60 m., of which 9 are navigable for the largest vessels, unites with James river, 37 m. above Point Comfort.
CHICKAMANGA, a river which takes its rise in the NW of the state of Georgia, U.S., enters the state of Tennessee, and falls into the river of that name, a few miles SSW of Hamilton.

CHICKAPEE, a river of the state of Massachusetts, U. S., which takes its rise in Worcester co., traverses a series of small lakes, and unites with the Connecticut in Hampden co., 5 m. N of Springfield. It has a total course of about 45 m. Its principal affluents are the Ware and Swift.

CHICKASAS, a tribe of North American Indians, who originally inhabited the country bounded by the Ohio on the N; on the E by a line drawn from the bend in the Cumberland river to the Muscle shoals of the Tennessee; on the S by the Choctas territory or what is now the state of Mississippi; and on the W by the Mississippi. Their chosen abodes were on the upland country which gives birth to the Yazoo and the Tombecbee, where the grass is verdant in midwinter, and if the earth be but carelessly gashed to receive the kernel of maize, the thick corn springs abundantly from the fertile soil. The occupants of this happy region were the most intrepid warriors of the S, and proved themselves the faithful and invincible allies of the English in their conflicts with the French. [Bancroft.] Their entire number does not now exceed 4,200. Some of the mixed bloods amongst them are intelligent, well-educated men: but as a people, the C. have made less progress in the arts of civilized life than the Choctaws.

CHICKASAW, a county in the state of Mississippi, U. S., comprising an area of 970 sq. m., watered by Yallo Busha river and Oktibbeha creek. Pop. in 1840, 2,955, of whom 2,148 were whites, 806 slaves, and 1 free-coloured.
CHICKASAWHA. See PASCAGOULA.

CHICKELEES, a river of Oregon. rising on the spurs of the Cascade range, 40 m. N of Columbia river, to the N of the head-streams of the Cowlitz, and flowing W into Gray's harbour. The land between the Cowlitz and the C. is an extensive prairie known as the Cammas plains. Fertile patches of alluvial land occur on the C. river.
CHICKELEY, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 110 m. WSW of Bejapore, district and 15 m. S of Kolapoor, on the Beyd Gunga, one of the headstreams of the Kistna.
CHICKERELL (WEST), a parish of Dorset, 31/4 m. NW of Weymouth. Area 1,250 acres. Pop. 531.
CHICKLADE, a parish of Wilts, 1 m. N of Hindon. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 109.
CHICKNEY, a parish of Essex, 3 m. SW of Thaxted. Area 680 acres. Pop. 59.

CHICKTAWAGA, a township of Erie co., in the state of New York, 6 m. E of Buffalo, watered by Cayuga and Cazenove creeks. It has an undulating surface, and is generally fertile. In the S it contains an Indian reservation belonging to the Seneca tribe. Pop. in 1840, 1,137. CHICKWARD, PEMBER'S OAK, AND LILWALL, a township of Herefordshire, in the p. of Kington, 21 m. W of Huntingdon. Pop. 380.

CHICLANA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, cap. of a judicial partido, in the prov. and 11 m. SSE of Cadiz, situated near the E bank of the channel of the Santi-Petri, opposite the Isla-de-Leon, and commanding a fine view of the bay and town of Cadiz. Pop. 7,144. It is a "small, mean, and dirty town, with scarcely a house of decent exterior" [Murray]; yet it is "the Highgate of the good citizens of Cadiz" [Scott], and contains 2 parish churches, 3 convents, an hospital, an alms-house, a posada, and a theatre. The environs are adorned with numerous villas, belonging to the inhabitants of Cadiz, and on an eminence are the ruins of a Moorish castle. There are some mineral waters in the neighbourhood.—Also a town in New Castile, in the prov. and 54 m. NE of Jaen, partido of Villacarrilo, and 27 m. ENE of La Carolina. The partido comprises only 3 pueblos or towns, viz.: Chiclana, Conil, and Vejer.

CHICO, a river of New Grenada, in the intendancy of Equador, which unites with the Ahuaricu, 10 m. above the junction of that river with the Coca. -Also a river of Patagonia, which is supposed to issue from a lake in the interior, and to fall into the estuary of the Santa Cruz, on the SE coast. CHICO, MAMORE, or SIPIRI, a river of Bolivia, in the intendancy of Santa Cruz, which falls into the Mamore, on the l. bank, in S lat. 15° 5'.
CHICONTEPEC, a town in the state of Vera Cruz, in Mexico. It is built on the barren rocky crest of the Sierra-Madre, on the road from Tampico to Mexico, by Zacualtipem.
CHICORA, a village on the Zambesi river, in E Africa, 15 days' beyond Tette.

CHICOREY, or CHICURI, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 100 m. WSW of Bejapore, district and 25 m. SE of Kolapur. It is surrounded by hills, and is intersected by a stream which forms a cascade in its passage through the town. The environs are noted for their grapes.

CHICOT, a county of the state of Arkansas, U.S., comprising a level area of 1,800 sq. m., drained by Bartholemew, Bouff, and Macon rivers. The soil differs in quality, and is partly very unproductive. Pop. in 1830, 1,165; in 1840, 3,806, of whom 1,105 were whites, 2,698 slaves, and 3 free-coloured. Cap., Columbia. See also DUPAS.

CHICOUTIMI, or Shekutimish, a river, and a trading-port, in Lower Canada. - The river is the largest tributary to the Saguenay. It flows out of Lake Kenwangomi, and joins the Saguenay on the r. bank, 65 m. above its entrance into the St. Lawrence. —The trading-port of C. stands on the W side of the river, at its confluence with the Saguenay. CHI

CHICOVA, a town of South Africa, cap. of a district of the same name, in Mono-Motapa, on the r. bank of the Zambeze, a little below the cataract of Cabrabassa, and 160 m. W of Tette, and about the same distance NE of Zumba. It possesses from its situation a considerable entrepot trade. The district produces large quantities of rice, and abounds with luxuriant pasturage. It has long been celebrated for its mines of silver, copper, and iron. Its manufactures of steel form an important branch of commerce between Sofala and India. An unsuccessful attempt to penetrate this territory was made by the Portuguese in the time of Sebastian I.

CHICULDAH, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwa, on the N bank of the Nerbudda, 40 m. SW of Maundoo. In 1820 it consisted of about 300 houses, with a ruinous ghurry, and surrounded by a mud wall.
CHIDAMER, a town of the island of Java, in the district of Chianjur, on a river of the same name, 90 m. SSE of Batavia.
CHIDDEN AND GLIDDEN, a tything of the p. of Hambledon, co. of Southampton. Pop. 207.
CHIDDINGFOLD, a parish of Surrey, 44 m. NE of Haslemere. Area 5,590 acres. Pop. 1,135.
CHIDDINGLEY, or CHITTINGLEIGH, a parish of Sussex, 5 m. NW of Hailsham. Area 5,200 acres. Pop. 930.
CHIDDINGSTONE, a parish of Kent, 6 m. WSW of Tunbridge, near the source of the Medway, and close to the South Eastern railway. Area 3,200 acres. Pop. 1,405.
CHIDEOCK, a parish of Dorset, 2 m. W of Bridport. Area 2,240 acres. Pop. 826.
CHIDES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Nievre, cant. of Luzy. Pop. 1,208.
CHIDHAM, a parish of Sussex, 5 m. WSW of Chichester. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 325.
CHIDLEY CAPE, a promontory of West Greenland, in Davis straits, to the S of Disco island, from which it is separated by SE bay.
CHILDLOV, a town of Russia in Europe, in the gov. and 107 m. NW of Vilna, district and 12 m. NNE of Rossieny.
CHIDLOW, a township of the p. of Malpas, Cheshire. Area 140 acres. Pop. 12.

CHIDRIAN (San), a town of Spain, in Old Castile, in the prov. and 24 m. NNE of Avila, partido of Arévalo, on the l. bank of the Boltoya, near its confluence with the Eresma. The environs are celebrated for their wine.

CHIEM-SEE, a lake of Bavaria, in the circle of Upper Bavaria. It lies between the Inn and the Alz, 13 m. S of Wasserburg. It is 35 m. in circumf. and in many parts 80 fath. deep. It has three islands on it. Its fisheries, which are extremely productive, belong to the Crown.
CHIEN (Au), a small river of Lower Canada, an affluent of the Saguenay.
CHIENS (Aux), a river of Lower Canada, in the seignory of Blainville, which runs NE past the village of St. Therese, and bending S, discharges itself into the St. Jean or Jesus.

CHIENTI, a river of the States-of-the-Church, which descends from the E side of the Apennines, in the delegation and 11 m. SW of Camerino, flows through the SE part of the delegation of Macerita, and throws itself into the Adriatic, 3 m. SE of Civita-Nuova, on the confines of the delegation of Fermo, and after a total course of about 54 m. It is the ancient Flusor.—Also a town of Naples, in the prov. of Capitanata, district and 18 m. NW of San- Severo, on the Adriatic. Its pop., amounting to 1,680, still to a great extent consists of Albanians, its original settlers.

CHIERI, or CHIERS, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandamento, in the prov. and 9 m. ESE of Turin, on the brow of a hill which is bathed by an affluent of the Grana. Pop. 12,000. It is enclosed by walls, -one of the gates of which is built in the style of a triumphal arch, - and contains 4 public squares, several handsome churches and convents, a communal college, 4 hospitals, and a lunatic asylum. The houses are tolerably well-built, and in the environs are, intermingled with vineyards, numerous handsome villas. A few spinning-mills and clothmanufactories constitute the chief branches of local industry. C., the Cairo of the middle ages, was formerly a place of considerable strength. CHIERS, CHOR, or KOR, a river which takes its rise on the frontier of Belgium, in the prov. of Luxemburg, to the NW of Esch, crosses the N part of the French dep. of the Moselle and Meuse, passing Longwy, Montmedy, and La Mouilly, thence bends N into the dep. of Ardennes; passes Carignan; and unites with the Meuse 5 m. SE of Sedan, after a total course of 60 m., of which about 18 are navigable.
CHIESA, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the gov. of Milan, delegation and 12 m. N of Sondrio, on the r. bank of the Malinco.

CHIESE, a river of Venetian Lombardy - the Cleusis of the Romans-which takes its rise on the E side of Monte Adamello, a spur of the Rhætian Alps, 25 m. W of Trent; directs its course with considerable sinuosities to the S; traverses Lago d'Idro and the Val Sabia; and, after having watered the E part of the delegation of Brescia, enters that of Mantua, and throws itself into the Oglio, on the l. bank, 2 m. SE of Canneto, passing in its course of about 90 m. Condino, Anfo Vestone, Montechiaro, and Asola. Its channel is rocky, and current impetuous. It abounds with pike and barbel.

CHIETI, a fortified town of Naples, cap. of the prov. of Abruzzo-Citra, situated on a hill, near the r. bank of the Pescara, 40 m. E of Aquila, and 100 m. N of Naples. Pop. 13,679. It is well-built; and contains a magnificent cathedral, 4 churches, several monasteries, a college, a theological seminary, a lyceum, a military hospital, several alms-houses, a theatre, and numerous remains of its ancient magnificence. It has some manufactories of woollen and silk fabrics, and a considerable trade in wine, corn, oil, and cloth. Fairs are held twice a-year. The origin of this town—the Teatea Marrucinorum of the Romans—is lost in antiquity. It was long subject to the Greeks. At the fall of the Roman empire, it passed into the hands of the Goths and Lombards; and at the conquest of the latter by Charlemagne, was besieged by Pepin and destroyed. It was subsequently rebuilt by the Normans. In 1802 it was taken by the French.—The district of C. comprises 8 communes, viz.: Bucchianico, Caramanico, Chieti, Francavilla, Guardiagrele, Manupello, Tollo, and Santo Valentino.
CHIEVELEY, or CHEVELY, a parish of Berks, 4½ m. N of Newbury. Area 9,410 acres. Pop. 1,936.
CHIEVO, a village of Venetian Lombardy, in the prov. and 2 m. NW of Verona, on the r. bank of the Adige. Pop. 686. It has some silk manufactories.

CHIEVRES, a town of Belgium, cap. of a cant. in the prov. of Hainault, 12 m. NW of Mons, near the confluence of the Hunel with the Dendre. Pop. in 1836, 3,163. It possesses a fine old church; and has extensive manufactories of cloth, pottery, and tiles, a salt-refinery, and several tanneries, breweries, and distilleries of gin. The commerce consists chiefly in grain and oil. A horse-fair takes place once a-year. This town, originally named Cervia, is of considerable antiquity. CHI

CHIFLIK, or CHIFTLIK, a sanjak and town of Turkish Armenia, in the pash. of Erzrum. The sanjak, which is intersected by the Niksar river, contains 60 villages. The town is situated in a low fertile plain, 15 m. W of Sadac, near the river. Its houses are built of stone, and it is interspersed with poplars and willows, and surrounded with gardens. Morier says the plain in which C. lies quite transported him into some of the best parts of England. The fields were parted off by hedges and ditches; the road was well-defined; and the villages were intermixed with the most luxuriant verdure.
CHIGIOGNA, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Tessin, 15 m. NNW of Bellinzona, in the Val Laventina, on the l. bank of the Tessin, about a m. SE of Faido.
CHIGNAL (St. James), a parish of Essex, 3 m. NW of Chelmsford, on the Chelmer. Area 930 acres. Pop. 252.
CHIGNAL-SMEALY, a parish of Essex, 42 m. NW of Chelmsford. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. 94.

CHIGNECTO BAY, the NE extremity of the bay of Fundy, between New Brunswick and Nova Scotia. It is divided from the Mines channel by a peninsula of which Cape C., in N lat. 45° 22', W long. 64° 491', is the W extremity. At a point 11 m. above Cape Euragee, which lies 12 or 13 m. within the bay on its N side, it divides into two branches,—one leading to Cumberland basin and the river Missiquash, which runs across the isthmus, and traces the remainder of the boundary line betwixt Nova Scotia and New Brunswick; and the other-sometimes called Beau basin—running in a N direction to the Petit Coudiac river. The former branch is navigable to within 13 m. of Verte bay in the gulf of St. Lawrence; and it is remarkable that when the rise of tide in the latter gulf is only 8 ft., it rises from 50 to 60 ft. in Cumberland basin. A scheme for a canal between C. bay and the gulf of St. Lawrence across this isthmus, and cut from River Au Lac to Tidnish river, about 11 m. in length, was reported against by Captain Crawley in 1843. At the mouths of all the rivers emptying into C. bay, there are extensive collections of alluvial matter, called salt-marshes, chiefly formed by the operation of marine currents. These diked marshes and meadows are highly fertile; and grazing is successfully followed by the settlers in this quarter, who send butter and cheese in great quantities to Halifax and St. John's.
